content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

August 26, 1993

Veronica A. Nebb
Meyers, Nave, Riback & Silver
50 Old Courthouse Square
Suite 603
Santa Rosa, CA 95404

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-93-228

Dear Ms. Nebb:

This letter confirms the telephone advice provided to
Fred Etheridge, formerly of your office, who had requested advice
on behalf of Healdsburg City Councilmember Pete Peterson
concerning his responsibilities under the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

QUESTIONS

1. Within the past 12 months, Councilmember Peterson had a
business relationship with Mr. Drew. Mr. Drew located a tenant
for a vacancy in an office building in which Councilmember
Peterson has an ownership interest. Councilmember Peterson paid
Mr. Drew a commission for Mr. Drew's services in locating the
tenant.

A. May Councilmember Peterson participate in making a
decision to acquire the Ratchford property if Mr. Drew represents
Mrs. Ratchford in the land transaction?

B. May Councilmember Peterson participate in making a
decision to acquire the Healdsburg Ridge and Digger's Bend
properties if Mr. Drew represents property owners in the land
transactions?

C. May Councilmember Peterson participate in making a
decision to annex Planning Area A and B properties to the city if
some of the property owners within the Planning Areas will be
represented by Mr. Drew in associated land transactions?

¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations section
18000 et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95812 • (916) 322-5660

Our File No. A-93-228
Page 2


     2.  Councilmember Peterson has an ownership interest in an
office building within 300 feet of a Northwestern Pacific Railroad
right-of-way.  May Councilmember Peterson participate in making
decisions to acquire the Northwestern Pacific Railroad right-of-
way?

     3.  The City of Healdsburg owns a parcel of property, now
vacant, that is over 300 feet but under 1200 feet, from the
Councilmember's office building.

     A.  May Councilmember Peterson participate in making
decisions concerning the use of the city-owned parcel?

     B.  How does Councilmember Peterson determine if a decision
will affect the fair market value of his office building by
$10,000?

                              CONCLUSIONS

     1.  A.,B.,C.  Mr. Drew is not a source of income to
Councilmember Peterson.  Rather, Councilmember Peterson is a
source of income to Mr. Drew.  As such, Mr. Drew is not a
potentially disqualifying "economic interest" and Councilmember
Peterson will not be precluded from participating in any decision
which affects Mr. Drew.

     2.  Councilmember Peterson may not participate in the
decision to acquire the Northwestern Pacific Railroad Right-of-Way
or any related decisions made in connection with that acquisition,
unless the decision will have no financial effect on his office
building.

     3.  A.  Councilmember Peterson may not participate in
decisions as to what use to put the vacant parcel of property, if
the decisions will increase or decrease the value of his office
building by $10,000, or affect the rental value of his property by
$1,000 per 12 month period.

     3.  B.  Because the regulation uses specific dollar amounts
to determine materiality, it is necessary for a public official to
make a reasoned good faith effort to determine the financial
effects of a decision on the official's interests in real
property.  A typical way to obtain such values is to use a real
estate appraiser or otherwise qualified agency staff person who
must consider, in pertinent part, the factors set forth in
Regulation 18702.3(d).

                                 FACTS

     Question 1 (A):  Fitch Mountain Open Space

     The City of Healdsburg is considering whether to purchase
tracts or real property on Fitch Mountain for open space

Our File No. A-93-228
Page 3


preservation. One of the parcels on Fitch Mountain being
considered by the city for acquisition is held by a
Mrs. Ratchford. Mrs. Ratchford will be represented in any real
property negotiations by a Mr. Eric Drew, a realtor. Mr. Drew
would likely receive a commission from Mrs. Ratchford if the
property is sold to the city.

     In the past 12 months, Mr. Drew located a tenant for a
vacancy in an office building in Healdsburg in which Councilmember
Peterson has an ownership interest. Councilmember Peterson paid
Mr. Drew a commission for Mr. Drew's services in locating the
tenant. The commission was paid in installments, with each
installment exceeding $250.00. The final commission installment
was made November 15, 1992.

     There are currently no plans between Mr. Drew and
Councilmember Peterson for a future business relationship.

Question 1 (B): Conservation Easement

     The city is considering acquiring a conservation easement or
fee title to property along Healdsburg Ridge and Digger's Bend
Russian River frontage. Some of the real property owners in these
two areas are represented by Mr. Drew. In the event the city did
decide to purchase some of these properties, it is likely Mr. Drew
would represent the property owners in the transactions and would
receive a commission for doing so.

Question 1 (C): Planning Areas A and B - Healdsburg North
Annexation

     The City of Healdsburg General Plan designates Planning Areas
A and B for possible future annexation into the city. If the
annexation of these properties does occur, they may be sold or
disposed of as part of the city's development plans. Mr. Drew
represents several real property owners within Planning Areas A
and B who could conceivably sell their properties as a result of
the annexation of their property into the city.

Question 2: Railroad Right of Way

     The office building in which Councilmember Peterson has an
ownership interest is located within 300 feet of a Northwestern
Pacific Railroad right-of-way. The city has agreed to participate
with various other governmental entities to acquire the
Northwestern Pacific Railroad right-of-way for the purpose of
continuing the commercial railroad along that right-of-way.
Various decisions in connection with that acquisition will be
before the Healdsburg City Council.

Question 3: Vacant City Property

     The city owns a parcel of property, now vacant, that is over
300 feet but under 1200 feet from Councilmember Peterson's office

Our File No. A-93-228
Page 4


building. The city council will be making decisions as to what
use to put the property. For example, one proposed use is to
construct a community services building on the site, and another
is to construct a public parking lot upon it. Councilmember
Peterson is aware of Regulation 18703.5 (set forth below) and of
the Chambers Advice Letter, No. A 93-035 concerning this property.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits a public official from making,
participating in making, or using his or her official position to
influence a governmental decision in which the official knows or
have reason to know the official has a financial interest. A
"public official" is defined in Section 82048 and Regulation 18700
as every natural person who is a member, officer, employee, or
consultant of a state and local government agency and would
include Healdsburg City Councilmembers.

Economic Interests

Section 87103 provides that a public official has a financial
interest in a governmental decision within the meaning of Section
87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have
a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on
the public generally, on the official, or on a member of the
official's immediate family, or on:

(a) Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

(b) Any real property in which the public
official has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

(c) Any source of income, other than gifts
and other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars ($250) or more in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.

(d) Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management.

(e) Any donor of, or any intermediary or
agent for a donor of, a gift or gifts aggregating
two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value
provided to, received by, or promised to the public

Our File No. A-93-228
Page 5


official within 12 months prior to the time when
the decision is made.

For purposes of this section, indirect
investment or interest means any investment or
interest owned by the spouse or dependent child of
a public official, by an agent on behalf of a
public official, or by a business entity or trust
in which the official, the official's agents,
spouse, and dependent children own directly,
indirectly, or beneficially a 10-percent interest
or greater.

Section 87103.

Accordingly, a public official may not make, participate in
making, or attempt to use his or her official position to
influence a governmental decision if the decision will have a
reasonably foreseeable and material financial effect,
distinguishable from the effect on the public generally, on the
official, or on a member of the official's immediate family, or on
any of the official's economic interests specified in Section
87103 above.

You stated that Councilmember Peterson owns an office
building. Thus, Councilmember Peterson has an interest in real
property assuming that the value of his interest in the building
is $1,000 or more. (Section 87103(b).) In addition, there are
tenants in the office building. If so, the tenants who make
payments to Councilmember Peterson pursuant to a lease or month-
to-month tenancy are a source of income to the councilmember.
(Section 87103(c).)

Consequently, any person or business that has been a source
of income of $250 or more to Councilmember Peterson within 12
months of the decision is a potentially disqualifying "economic
interest" as defined in Section 87103.

Mr. Drew does not fall in this category or in any of the
categories set forth in Section 87103. Mr. Drew is not a source
of income to Councilmember Peterson. Rather, Councilmember
Peterson is a source of income to Mr. Drew. As such, Mr. Drew is
not a potentially disqualifying "economic interest" and
Councilmember Peterson will not be precluded from participating in
any decision which affects Mr. Drew.

On the other hand, Councilmember Peterson's interest in the
office building is a potentially disqualifying "economic interest"
and he will be precluded from participating in any decision if the
decision will have a reasonably foreseeable and material financial
effect on this real property interest.

Our File No. A-93-228
Page 6


     The effects of a decision are reasonably foreseeable if there
is a substantial likelihood that they will occur. To be foresee-
able, the effects of a decision must be more than a mere
possibility; however, certainty is not required. (Downey Cares v.
Downey Community Development Com. (1987) 196 Cal.App.3d 983,
989-991; Witt v. Morrow (1977) 70 Cal.App.3d 817, 822; In re
Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.) The Act seeks to prevent more
than actual conflicts of interest; it seeks to prevent even the
appearance of a possible conflict of interest. (Witt v. Morrow,
supra at 823.)

     It is foreseeable that the decision to acquire the
Northwestern Pacific Railroad right-of-way and other decisions
made in connection with that acquisition will have a foreseeable
financial effect on Councilmember Peterson's office building which
is located within 300 feet of the right-of-way. Likewise, it is
foreseeable that the decision(s) as to what use to put the parcel
of vacant property that is between 300 and 1200 feet of the office
building will also have a financial effect on the building.

     The issue is whether the financial effect of the decision on
the office building in each of those situations is material.
Regulation 18702 sets forth the guidelines for determining whether
an official's economic interest in a decision is "materially"
affected as required by Section 87103.

     If the official's economic interest is directly involved in
the decision, then Regulation 18702.1 applies to determine
materiality.2 If the official's economic interest is not directly
involved in the decision but is indirectly affected by the
decision, or if the effect of the decision is not material under

2    Regulation 18702.1(b) states:

     A person or business entity is directly involved in
a decision before an official's agency when that
person or entity, either personally or by an agent:

          (1) Initiates the proceeding in which the
decision will be made by filing an application,
claim, appeal, or similar request or;

          (2) Is a named party in, or is the subject
of, the proceeding concerning the decision before
the official or the official's agency.

          (3) A person or business entity is the
subject of a proceeding if a decision involves the
issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation
of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or
contract with, the subject person or business
entity.

Our File No. A-93-228
Page 7


Section 18702.1, then it must be determined if the effect is
material under Regulation 18702.3.

Regulation 18702.3 provides, in pertinent part:

(a) The effect of a decision is material as
to real property in which an official has a direct,
indirect or beneficial ownership interest (not
including a leasehold interest), if any of the
following applies:

(1) The real property in which the official
has an interest, or any part of that real property,
is located within a 300 foot radius of the
boundaries (or the proposed boundaries) of the
property which is the subject of the decision,
unless the decision will have no financial effect
upon the official's real property interest.

(2) The decision involves construction of, or
improvements to, streets, water, sewer, storm
drainage or similar facilities, and the real
property in which the official has an interest will
receive new or substantially improved services.

(3) The real property in which the official
has an interest is located outside a radius of 300
feet and any part of the real property is located
within a radius of 2,500 feet of the boundaries (or
the proposed boundaries) of the property which is
the subject of the decision and the decision will
have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect of:

(A) Ten thousand dollars ($10,000) or more on
the fair market value of the real property in which
the official has an interest; or

(B) Will affect the rental value of the
property by $1,000 or more per 12 month period.

Accordingly, Councilmember Peterson may not participate in
the decision to acquire the Northwestern Pacific Railroad Right-
of-Way or any related decisions made in connection with that
acquisition, unless the decision will have no financial effect on
the office building. Considering the fact that the purpose of the
acquisition is to continue the commercial railroad along the
right-of-way which is within 300 feet of the councilmember's
office building, it seems likely that there will be a financial
effect on the fair market value of the building or the rental
value of the property.

Similarly, Councilmember Peterson may not participate in
decisions as to what use to put the vacant parcel of property

Our File No. A-93-228
Page 8


which is within 300-1200 feet of the councilmember's office
building, if the decisions will increase or decrease the fair
market value of his office building by $10,000, or affect the
rental value of his property by $1,000 per 12 month period. (See,
Chambers Advice Letter, No. A-93-035.)

     Because the regulation uses specific dollar amounts to
determine materiality, it is necessary for a public official to
make a reasonably good faith effort to determine the financial
effects of a decision on his interests in real property. A
typical way to obtain such values is to use a real estate
appraiser or otherwise qualified agency staff person.

     As pointed out in the Chambers letter, supra, factors which
must be considered by the appraiser in determining whether a
decision will have a material financial effect on an official's
real property include, but are not limited to:

     (1) The proximity of the property which is the
subject of the decision and the magnitude of the
property project or change in use in relationship to the
property in which the official has an interest;

     (2) Whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will affect the development potential or income
producing potential of the property;

     (3) In addition to the foregoing, in the case of
residential property, whether it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will result in a change to
the character of the neighborhood including, but not
limited to, effects on traffic, view, privacy, intensity
of use, noise levels, air emissions, or similar traits
of the neighborhood.

                              (Regulation 18702.3(d).)

     In addition, it is critical to educate the person performing
the appraisal of the standards for evaluation. For example, the
facts and analysis on which the appraisal is based must be
thoroughly documented. The appraisal should provide dollar
amounts for either fair market values or rental values, as
applicable. (Gelb Advice Letter, No. A-92-138; Hicks Advice
Letter, A-92-492.; and Murphy Advice Letter, No. A-92-559; copies
enclosed.)

Our File No. A-93-228
Page 9


I trust this letter answers all your questions.3

Sincerely,

Wayne Ordos
Executive Director

Deanne Stone

By: Deanne Stone
Senior Commission Counsel

Enclosures




3 Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries. Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advise letters may be obtained from the commission at a
cost of 10¢ per page.

or other entitlement to,
or
contract with, the subject person or business
entity.


Our File No.
A-93—228
Section 18702.1, then it must be determined if the effect is
material under Regulation 18702.3.
Regulation 18702.3 provides,
in pertinent part:
(a)
The effect of a decision is material as
to real property in which an official has a direct,
indirect or beneficial ownership interest (not
including a leasehold interest), if any of the
following applies:
(1)
The real property in which the official
has an interest,
or any part of that real property,
is located within a 300 foot radius of the
boundaries
(or the proposed boundaries)
of the
property which is the subject of the decision,
unless the decision will have no financial effect
upon the official’s real property interest.
(2)
The decision involves construction of,
or
improvements to, streets, water,
sewer,
storm
drainage or similar facilities,
and the real
property in which the official has an interest will
receive new or substantially improved services.
(3)
The real property in which the official
has an interest is located outside a radius of 300
feet and any part of the real property is located
within a radius of 2,500 feet of the boundaries
(or
the proposed boundaries)
of the property which is
the subject of the decision and the decision will
have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect of:
(A)
Ten thousand dollars
($10,000)
or more on
the fair market value of the real property in which
the official has an interest; or
(B)
Will affect the rental value of the
property by $1,000 or more per 12 month period.
Accordingly,
Councilmember Peterson may not participate in
the decision to acquire the Northwestern Pacific Railroad Right-
of—Way or any related decisions made in connection with that
acquisition, unless the decision will have no financial effect on
the office building.
Considering the fact that the purpose of the
acquisition is to continue the commercial railroad along the
right-of-way which is within 300 feet of the councilxnember’s
office building, it seems likely that there will be a financial
effect on the fair market value of the building or the rental
value of the property.
Similarly,
Councilmember Peterson may not participate in
decisions as to what use to put the vacant parcel of property


Our Page
8
which is within 300—1200 feet of the councilmember’s office
building, if the decisions will increase or decrease the fair
market value of his office building by $10,000, or affect the
rental value of his property by $1,000 per 12 month period.
(See,
Chambers Advice Letter,
No. A-93-035.)
Because the regulation uses specific dollar amounts to
determine materiality, it is necessary for a public official to
make a reasonable good faith effort to determine the financial
effects of a decision on his interests in real property.
A
ty$ical way to obtain such values is to use a real estate
appraiser or otherwise qualified agency staff person.
As pointed out in the Chambers letter, supra, factors which
must be considered by the appraiser in determining whether a
decision will have a material financial effect on an official’s
real property include,
but are not limited to:
(1)
The proximity of the property which is the
subject of the decision and the magnitude of the
proposed project or change in use in relationship to the
property in which the official has an interest;
(2)
Whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will affect the development potential or income
producing potential of the property;
(3)
In addition to the foregoing,
in the case of
residential property, whether it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will result in a change to
the character of the neighborhood including,
but not
limited to, effects on traffic,
view, privacy, intensity
of use,
noise levels, air emissions,
or similar traits
of the neighborhood.
(Regulation 18702.3 (d).)
In addition, it is critical to educate the person performing
the appraisal of the standards for evaluation.
For example, the
facts and analysis on which the appraisal is based must be
thoroughly documented.
The appraisal should provide dollar
amounts for either fair market values or rental values,
as
applicable.
(Geib Advice Letter,
No.
A-92-138; Hicks Advice
Letter, A-92-492.;
and Murphy Advice Letter,
No. A-92-559; copies
enclosed.)


Our Page
9
I trust this letter answers all your questions.
3

QUESTION: 1.
Within the past 12 months,
Councilmember Peterson had a
business relationship with Mr.
Drew.
Mr.
Drew located a tenant
for a vacancy in an office building in which Councilmember
Peterson has an ownership interest.
Councilmember Peterson paid
Mr.
Drew a commission for Mr.
Drew’s services in locating the
tenant.
A.
May Councilinember Peterson participate in making a
decision to acquire the Ratchford property if Mr.
Drew represents
Mrs. Ratchford in the land transaction?
B.
May Councilmeinber Peterson participate in making a
decision to acquire the Healdsburg Ridge and Digger’s Bend
properties if Mr.
Drew represents property owners in the land
transactions?
C.
May Councilmember Peterson participate in making a
decision to annex Planning Area A and B properties to the city if
some of the property owners within the Planning Areas will be
represented by Mr.
Drew in associated land transactions?
et seq.

Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2
2.
Councilmember Peterson has an ownership interest in an
office building within 300 feet of a Northwestern Pacific Railroad
right-of-way.
May Councilmember Peterson participate in making
decisions to acquire the Northwestern Pacific Railroad right-of—
way?
3.
The City of Healdsburg owns a parcel of property,
now
vacant, that is over 300 feet but under 1200 feet,
from the
Councilmember’s office building.
A.
May Councilmember Peterson participate in making
decisions concerning the use of the city—owned parcel?
B.
How does Councilmember Peterson determine if a decision
will affect the fair market value of his office building by
$10, 000?

CONCLUSION: 1.
A.,B.,C.
Mr.
Drew is not a source of income to
Councilmember Peterson.
Rather,
Councilmember Peterson is a
source of income to Mr.
Drew.
As such,
Mr.
Drew is not a
potentially disqualifying “economic interest” and Councilmember
Peterson will not be precluded from participating in any decision
which affects Mr.
Drew.
2.
Councilmember Peterson may not participate in the
decision to acquire the Northwestern Pacific Railroad Right-of-Way
or any related decisions made in connection with that acquisition,
unless the decision will have no financial effect on his office
building.
3.
A.
Councilmember Peterson may not participate in
decisions as to what use to put the vacant parcel of property, if
the decisions will increase or decrease the value of his office
building by $10,000,
or affect the rental value of his property by
$1,000 per
12 month period.
3.
B.
Because the regulation uses specific dollar amounts
to determine materiality, it is necessary for a public official to
make a reasonable good faith effort to determine the financial
effects of a decision on the official’s interests in real
property.
A typical way to obtain such values is to use a real
estate appraiser or otherwise qualified agency staff person who
must consider,
in pertinent part, the factors set forth in
Regulation 18702.3(d).
embedding.first_500_words: California Fair Political Practices Commission August 26, 1993 Veronica A. Nebb Meyers, Nave, Riback & Silver 50 Old Courthouse Square Suite 603 Santa Rosa, CA 95404 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-93-228 Dear Ms. Nebb: This letter confirms the telephone advice provided to Fred Etheridge, formerly of your office, who had requested advice on behalf of Healdsburg City Councilmember Pete Peterson concerning his responsibilities under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ QUESTIONS 1. Within the past 12 months, Councilmember Peterson had a business relationship with Mr. Drew. Mr. Drew located a tenant for a vacancy in an office building in which Councilmember Peterson has an ownership interest. Councilmember Peterson paid Mr. Drew a commission for Mr. Drew's services in locating the tenant. A. May Councilmember Peterson participate in making a decision to acquire the Ratchford property if Mr. Drew represents Mrs. Ratchford in the land transaction? B. May Councilmember Peterson participate in making a decision to acquire the Healdsburg Ridge and Digger's Bend properties if Mr. Drew represents property owners in the land transactions? C. May Councilmember Peterson participate in making a decision to annex Planning Area A and B properties to the city if some of the property owners within the Planning Areas will be represented by Mr. Drew in associated land transactions? ¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations section 18000 et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations. 428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95812 • (916) 322-5660 Our File No. A-93-228 Page 2 2. Councilmember Peterson has an ownership interest in an office building within 300 feet of a Northwestern Pacific Railroad right-of-way. May Councilmember Peterson participate in making decisions to acquire the Northwestern Pacific Railroad right-of- way? 3. The City of Healdsburg owns a parcel of property, now vacant, that is over 300 feet but under 1200 feet, from the Councilmember's office building. A. May Councilmember Peterson participate in making decisions concerning the use of the city-owned parcel? B. How does Councilmember Peterson determine if a decision will affect the fair market value of his office building by $10,000? CONCLUSIONS 1. A.,B.,C. Mr. Drew is not a source of income to Councilmember Peterson. Rather, Councilmember Peterson is a source of income to Mr. Drew. As such, Mr. Drew is not a potentially disqualifying "economic interest" and Councilmember Peterson will not be precluded from participating in any decision which affects Mr. Drew. 2. Councilmember Peterson may not participate in the decision to acquire the Northwestern Pacific Railroad Right-of-Way or any related decisions made in connection with that acquisition, unless the decision will have no financial effect on his office building. 3. A. Councilmember Peterson may not participate in decisions as to what use to put the vacant parcel of property, if the decisions will increase
